content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers Chapter 12

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 12:1 -- "on account of the Cushite woman whom he had taken" vs. "because of the Cushite woman he had married" (al-odot ha-ishah ha-Kushit asher laqakh ki ishah Kushit laqakh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-spoke Miriam and-Aaron against Moses on-account of the-Cushite woman whom he-had-taken; for a-Cushite woman he-had-taken." |
| LOGOS | "And Miriam and Aaron spoke against Moses on account of the Cushite woman whom he had taken, for he had taken a Cushite woman." |
| PNEUMA | "Miriam and Aaron spoke against Moses on account of the Cushite woman he had married -- for he had taken a Cushite wife." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Miriam and Aaron spoke against Moses on account of the Cushite woman whom he had taken, for he had taken a Cushite woman." -- (a) EMET uses hyphenated gloss style; the consensus follows standard English prose. (b) PNEUMA renders laqakh as "married" in the first clause; while laqakh ishah can mean "to take a wife / to marry," the Hebrew verb is literally "took" (laqakh), and both EMET and LOGOS preserve the literal "taken." (c) PNEUMA omits the relative pronoun "whom" (asher). (d) PNEUMA uses "wife" in the second clause where the Hebrew has ishah ("woman"); while ishah can mean "wife," the consensus preserves "woman" to match the Hebrew's emphasis on the Cushite woman's identity rather than the marital relationship. (e) PNEUMA uses a dash for the causal clause where the Hebrew has ki ("for"). (f) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" -- the Hebrew vatdabber begins with the waw-consecutive, which the binding convention requires preserving.

---

### 12:2 -- "Has YHWH indeed spoken only through Moses?" vs. "Has YHWH spoken only through Moses?" (ha-raq akh be-Mosheh dibber YHWH ha-lo gam banu dibber)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-they-said: 'Is-it-only through Moses [that] spoke YHWH? Has-not also through-us he-spoke?' And-heard YHWH." |
| LOGOS | "And they said, 'Has YHWH indeed spoken only through Moses? Has he not also spoken through us?' And YHWH heard." |
| PNEUMA | "They said, 'Has YHWH spoken only through Moses? Has he not spoken through us as well?' And YHWH heard it." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they said, 'Has YHWH indeed spoken only through Moses? Has he not also spoken through us?' And YHWH heard." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention for va-yomeru). (b) LOGOS includes "indeed" to render the emphatic double particle ha-raq akh; PNEUMA omits this intensification; EMET renders it as "Is-it-only." The consensus includes "indeed" following LOGOS to preserve the emphatic force of the Hebrew particles. (c) PNEUMA renders gam banu as "through us as well" rather than "also through us"; both are valid, but "also" maps more directly to gam. (d) PNEUMA adds "it" to "And YHWH heard" -- the Hebrew has no object suffix (va-yishma YHWH is absolute: "And YHWH heard"), so the consensus omits "it." (e) EMET preserves Hebrew word order ("through Moses spoke YHWH"); the consensus follows English natural order with LOGOS.

---

### 12:3 -- "more than any man who was on the face of the ground" vs. "more than any person on the face of the earth" (anav me'od mi-kol ha-adam asher al penei ha-adamah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-the-man Moses [was] very humble, more-than-all the-man who [was] upon the-face of the-ground." |
| LOGOS | "And the man Moses was very humble, more than any man who was on the face of the ground." |
| PNEUMA | "Now the man Moses was more humble than any person on the face of the earth." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the man Moses was very humble, more than any man who was on the face of the ground." -- (a) PNEUMA opens with "Now" instead of "And" (the Hebrew ve-ha-ish begins with ve-, "and"; binding convention). (b) PNEUMA renders ha-adam as "person" rather than "man"; the Hebrew adam can mean "human being" generically, but both EMET and LOGOS render it "man" (the standard translation in this context), and the consensus follows. (c) PNEUMA uses "earth" for adamah; the Hebrew adamah specifically means "ground/soil" (as distinct from erets, "earth/land"). EMET and LOGOS preserve "ground." (d) PNEUMA omits the relative clause marker "who was" (asher), condensing the syntax. (e) PNEUMA restructures the word order: "was more humble than" instead of "was very humble, more than" -- the Hebrew separates anav me'od ("humble very") from the comparative mi-kol ("more than all"); LOGOS preserves this two-part structure. (f) EMET has "more-than-all the-man" (preserving the Hebrew kol ha-adam); LOGOS/PNEUMA render "any man/person," which is the natural English equivalent of the superlative.

---

### 12:4 -- "And YHWH said suddenly" vs. "Suddenly, YHWH said" (va-yomer YHWH pit'om)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-said YHWH suddenly unto Moses and-unto Aaron and-unto Miriam: 'Go-out, [you] three of you, unto the-Tent of Meeting.' And-went-out [the] three of them." |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH said suddenly to Moses and to Aaron and to Miriam, 'Go out, you three, to the tent of meeting.' And the three of them went out." |
| PNEUMA | "Suddenly, YHWH said to Moses, to Aaron, and to Miriam, 'Come out, the three of you, to the Tent of Meeting.' So the three of them went out." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And YHWH said suddenly to Moses and to Aaron and to Miriam, 'Go out, you three, to the Tent of Meeting.' And the three of them went out." -- (a) PNEUMA fronts "Suddenly" to the beginning of the sentence and omits "And" (binding convention for va-yomer). (b) PNEUMA renders tse'u as "Come out" rather than "Go out"; while both are possible for the Hebrew imperative of yatsa, "Go out" is more precise for a directive to leave one's current location (EMET/LOGOS). (c) PNEUMA uses "So" instead of "And" for va-yets'u (binding convention). (d) LOGOS has lowercase "tent of meeting"; EMET has "Tent of Meeting." The consensus capitalizes both words as it is a proper designation for the sacred structure (Ohel Mo'ed). (e) PNEUMA uses comma-separated list ("to Moses, to Aaron, and to Miriam") where the Hebrew repeats the preposition el before each name; EMET/LOGOS preserve "and to Aaron and to Miriam" with the polysyndeton.

---

### 12:5 -- "and he called Aaron and Miriam, and the two of them went out" vs. "He called out, 'Aaron! Miriam!' -- and the two of them stepped forward"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-came-down YHWH in-a-pillar of cloud, and-stood [at]-the-entrance of the-Tent; and-called Aaron and-Miriam; and-went-out both-of-them." |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH came down in a pillar of cloud and stood at the entrance of the tent, and he called Aaron and Miriam, and the two of them went out." |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH came down in a pillar of cloud and stood at the entrance of the tent. He called out, 'Aaron! Miriam!' -- and the two of them stepped forward." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And YHWH came down in a pillar of cloud and stood at the entrance of the tent, and he called Aaron and Miriam, and the two of them went out." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention for va-yered). (b) PNEUMA dramatizes the calling by inserting direct speech ("'Aaron! Miriam!'") and an exclamatory format; the Hebrew va-yiqra Aharon u-Miryam is reported speech ("and he called Aaron and Miriam"), not direct address. (c) PNEUMA renders va-yets'u shneihem as "the two of them stepped forward" rather than "the two of them went out"; the Hebrew yatsa means "to go out," not "to step forward." EMET has "both-of-them" for shneihem; LOGOS has "the two of them." The consensus follows LOGOS. (d) PNEUMA breaks the verse into two sentences, losing the continuous chain of waw-consecutive clauses that convey the rapid sequence of events.

---

### 12:6 -- "Hear now my words" vs. "Listen carefully to my words" / "If there is a prophet among you" vs. "If there shall be your prophet" (shim'u na devarai im yihyeh nevi'akhem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-he-said: 'Hear now my-words: If there-shall-be your-prophet, [I] YHWH in-the-vision unto-him I-make-myself-known; in-the-dream I-speak to-him.'" |
| LOGOS | "And he said, 'Hear now my words: If there is a prophet among you, I YHWH make myself known to him in a vision; in a dream I speak with him.'" |
| PNEUMA | "He said, 'Listen carefully to my words. If there is a prophet among you, I, YHWH, make myself known to him in a vision. I speak with him in a dream.'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And he said, 'Hear now my words: If there is a prophet among you, I YHWH make myself known to him in a vision; in a dream I speak with him.'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" (binding convention for va-yomer). (b) PNEUMA renders shim'u na as "Listen carefully" rather than "Hear now"; the Hebrew na is a particle of entreaty/urgency ("please/now"), not an adverb meaning "carefully." EMET/LOGOS both have "Hear now." (c) PNEUMA breaks the verse into separate sentences; the Hebrew presents a continuous conditional statement that EMET/LOGOS preserve with colons and semicolons. (d) EMET preserves the possessive "your-prophet" (nevi'akhem, literally "your prophet"); LOGOS/PNEUMA render "a prophet among you," which is the standard English equivalent. The consensus follows LOGOS. (e) PNEUMA reverses the final clause order: "I speak with him in a dream" rather than "in a dream I speak with him." The Hebrew fronts ba-khalom ("in a dream") for emphasis, creating a parallel with ba-mar'ah ("in a vision"); the consensus preserves this fronted structure. (f) EMET has "I-speak to-him" (bo); LOGOS has "I speak with him." The Hebrew be- can mean either "with" or "to"; the consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 12:7 -- "in all my house he is faithful" vs. "He is entrusted with my entire household" (be-khol beiti ne'eman hu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Not so my-servant Moses; in-all my-house faithful he." |
| LOGOS | "Not so with my servant Moses; in all my house he is faithful." |
| PNEUMA | "Not so with my servant Moses. He is entrusted with my entire household." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Not so with my servant Moses; in all my house he is faithful." -- (a) PNEUMA restructures the clause: "He is entrusted with my entire household" reinterprets the Hebrew be-khol beiti ne'eman hu ("in all my house faithful he [is]") by converting the locative phrase "in all my house" into the object of "entrusted with" and rendering bayit as "household" rather than "house." While the interpretation is defensible, it departs from the Hebrew syntax; "in all my house he is faithful" preserves the original structure (EMET/LOGOS). (b) PNEUMA renders "my entire household" for beiti; EMET/LOGOS have "my house." The Hebrew khol beiti is "all my house"; "entire household" is a paraphrase. (c) EMET omits "with" after "Not so" -- the Hebrew lo khen avdi Mosheh could be read either way; LOGOS/PNEUMA include "with" for clarity, and the consensus follows.

---

### 12:8 -- "Mouth to mouth" vs. "face to face" / "and in appearance" vs. "and by sight" vs. "openly" / "Why then were you not afraid" vs. "How then did you dare" (peh el peh adabber bo u-mar'eh ve-lo be-khidot u-temunat YHWH yabbit u-maddua lo yeretem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Mouth unto mouth I-speak to-him, and-[in]-appearance and-not [in]-riddles; and-the-form of YHWH he-beholds. And-why were-you-not afraid to-speak against-my-servant, against-Moses?" |
| LOGOS | "Mouth to mouth I speak with him, and by sight, and not in riddles, and the form of YHWH he beholds. Why then were you not afraid to speak against my servant, against Moses?" |
| PNEUMA | "With him I speak face to face -- openly, not in riddles -- and he beholds the very form of YHWH. How then did you dare to speak against my servant, against Moses?" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Mouth to mouth I speak with him, and in appearance, and not in riddles, and the form of YHWH he beholds. Why then were you not afraid to speak against my servant, against Moses?" -- (a) PNEUMA renders peh el peh as "face to face"; the Hebrew distinctly says "mouth to mouth" (peh = mouth), which is a different phrase from panim el panim ("face to face," Exod 33:11). The consensus preserves the MT's distinctive formulation (EMET/LOGOS). (b) For u-mar'eh: EMET has "and-[in]-appearance," LOGOS has "and by sight," PNEUMA has "openly." The Hebrew mar'eh (from ra'ah, "to see") means "sight/appearance/that which is seen." "In appearance" is closest to the Hebrew noun; "openly" (PNEUMA) is interpretive; "by sight" (LOGOS) is acceptable. The consensus uses "in appearance." (c) PNEUMA adds "very" before "form of YHWH" (not in the Hebrew). (d) PNEUMA restructures: "he beholds the very form of YHWH" (subject-verb-object) where the Hebrew has u-temunat YHWH yabbit ("and the form of YHWH he beholds," object-subject-verb). EMET/LOGOS preserve the Hebrew fronted-object structure. (e) PNEUMA renders u-maddua lo yeretem as "How then did you dare" rather than "Why then were you not afraid"; the Hebrew uses maddua ("why") and lo yeretem ("you were not afraid," from yare', "to fear"). PNEUMA's "dare" collapses the negative + "fear" into a single positive verb, losing the explicit reference to fear/reverence that is central to YHWH's rebuke. EMET/LOGOS preserve "were you not afraid." (f) PNEUMA uses dashes to set off the middle clauses, restructuring the verse into a different rhythm from the Hebrew's chain of waw-consecutive clauses. (g) EMET has "unto" for el; the consensus uses "to."

---

### 12:9 -- "And the anger of YHWH was kindled against them, and he departed" vs. "YHWH's anger burned against them, and he departed" (va-yikhar af YHWH bam va-yelakh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-burned the-anger of YHWH against-them, and-he-departed." |
| LOGOS | "And the anger of YHWH was kindled against them, and he went away." |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH's anger burned against them, and he departed." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the anger of YHWH was kindled against them, and he departed." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention for va-yikhar). (b) PNEUMA uses the possessive form "YHWH's anger" rather than "the anger of YHWH"; while both convey the same meaning, the construct chain af YHWH ("anger of YHWH") is better represented by the "of" construction (EMET/LOGOS). (c) For va-yikhar: EMET/PNEUMA have "burned," LOGOS has "was kindled." The idiom kharah af is conventionally rendered "anger was kindled" in English biblical translation, and "kindled" was used in the consensus for NUM 011:1, 10. The consensus follows LOGOS for consistency. (d) For va-yelakh: EMET/PNEUMA have "departed," LOGOS has "went away." The consensus uses "departed" as a more fitting rendering for a theophanic withdrawal.

---

### 12:10 -- "And the cloud departed from upon the tent" vs. "When the cloud lifted from above the tent" (ve-he-anan sar me-al ha-Ohel)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-the-cloud departed from-upon the-Tent; and-behold, Miriam [was] leprous as-snow; and-turned Aaron unto Miriam, and-behold [she was] leprous." |
| LOGOS | "And the cloud turned aside from upon the tent, and behold, Miriam was leprous, like snow. And Aaron turned toward Miriam, and behold, she was leprous." |
| PNEUMA | "When the cloud lifted from above the tent -- there was Miriam, afflicted with a skin disease white as snow! Aaron turned toward Miriam and saw that she was diseased." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the cloud departed from upon the tent, and behold, Miriam was leprous, like snow. And Aaron turned toward Miriam, and behold, she was leprous." -- (a) PNEUMA opens with "When" instead of "And" (binding convention for ve-he-anan; the Hebrew begins with the conjunction ve-). (b) PNEUMA renders sar as "lifted" rather than "departed" (EMET) or "turned aside" (LOGOS). The Hebrew sur means "to turn aside / to depart / to remove oneself"; "departed" captures the verb adequately. "Lifted" implies upward movement not specified in the Hebrew. (c) PNEUMA renders metsora'at as "afflicted with a skin disease" rather than "leprous" (EMET/LOGOS). While tsara'at is not identical to modern Hansen's disease, "leprous" is the longstanding conventional translation of metsora'at. The consensus retains the traditional rendering. (d) PNEUMA replaces both instances of ve-hinneh ("and behold") with dramatized narration ("there was Miriam" and "saw that she was diseased"), losing the Hebrew's deliberate double hinneh that conveys the shock of discovery. (e) PNEUMA adds an exclamation mark after "snow!" not warranted by the Hebrew. (f) PNEUMA omits the second "And" before "Aaron turned" (binding convention for va-yifen). (g) LOGOS has "turned aside" for sar; EMET has "departed." The verb sur can mean either; the consensus uses "departed" for clarity. (h) PNEUMA replaces the second "leprous" with "diseased," losing the deliberate Hebrew repetition of metsora'at.

---

### 12:11 -- "Oh, my lord, please do not lay sin upon us" vs. "Please, my lord! Do not hold against us the sin" (bi adoni al na tasheth aleinu khatta'at asher no'alnu va-asher khatanu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-said Aaron unto Moses: 'Please, my-lord, do-not lay upon-us sin in-which we-were-foolish and-in-which we-sinned.'" |
| LOGOS | "And Aaron said to Moses, 'Oh, my lord, please do not lay sin upon us, in which we have acted foolishly and in which we have sinned.'" |
| PNEUMA | "Aaron said to Moses, 'Please, my lord! Do not hold against us the sin we committed so foolishly.'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Aaron said to Moses, 'Oh, my lord, please do not lay sin upon us, in which we have acted foolishly and in which we have sinned.'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention for va-yomer). (b) PNEUMA renders bi adoni as "Please, my lord!" with an exclamation mark; EMET has "Please, my-lord"; LOGOS has "Oh, my lord." The particle bi is an entreaty often rendered "Oh" or "Please" -- the consensus follows LOGOS. (c) PNEUMA renders al na tasheth aleinu khatta'at as "Do not hold against us the sin"; the Hebrew verb shit means "to set/lay/place," not "to hold." "Lay sin upon us" (EMET/LOGOS) is more precise. (d) PNEUMA collapses the two relative clauses (asher no'alnu va-asher khatanu -- "in which we were foolish and in which we sinned") into a single phrase: "the sin we committed so foolishly." This loses the Hebrew's deliberate pairing of two distinct confessions -- foolishness (no'alnu) and sin (khatanu) -- as separate acts. It also adds "so" (not in the Hebrew) and omits the second asher clause entirely. The consensus preserves the two-clause structure (EMET/LOGOS).

---

### 12:12 -- "Let her not be as one dead" vs. "Do not let her be like a stillborn child" (al na tehi ka-met asher be-tse'to me-rekhem immo va-ye'akhel khatsi besaro)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Let-her-not be as-the-dead, who when-he-comes-out from-the-womb of his-mother, and-is-consumed half his-flesh." |
| LOGOS | "Let her not be as one dead, who, when he comes out of his mother's womb, half his flesh is consumed." |
| PNEUMA | "Do not let her be like a stillborn child, whose flesh is already half-consumed when it comes from its mother's womb!" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Let her not be as one dead, who, when he comes out of his mother's womb, half his flesh is consumed." -- (a) PNEUMA renders ka-met as "a stillborn child" rather than "one dead" / "the dead" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew met simply means "dead one" or "corpse"; "stillborn child" is the LXX's interpretation (ektrōma, "miscarriage"), not the MT's wording. The consensus preserves the Hebrew's broader term. (b) PNEUMA adds "already" (not in the Hebrew). (c) PNEUMA restructures the clause order, placing "when it comes from its mother's womb" after "half-consumed," reversing the Hebrew sequence. (d) PNEUMA changes the masculine pronouns ("he/his") to neuter ("it/its"), losing the Hebrew's masculine generic (which uses masculine pronouns for the dead one regardless of gender). (e) PNEUMA adds "whose flesh" with a possessive relative, restructuring the Hebrew's paratactic construction. (f) PNEUMA adds an exclamation mark not in the Hebrew. (g) EMET has "as-the-dead" (ka-met with the article); LOGOS has "as one dead" (a better English rendering). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 12:13 -- "O God, please heal her, please!" vs. "God -- please -- heal her!" (El na refa na lah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-cried Moses unto YHWH, saying: 'God, please, heal please to-her!'" |
| LOGOS | "And Moses cried out to YHWH, saying, 'O God, please heal her, please.'" |
| PNEUMA | "Moses cried out to YHWH, 'God -- please -- heal her!'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Moses cried out to YHWH, saying, 'O God, please heal her, please!'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention for va-yits'aq). (b) PNEUMA omits le'mor ("saying"), which is present in the Hebrew and preserved by EMET/LOGOS. (c) PNEUMA renders the double na with dashes for staccato effect ("God -- please -- heal her!"), omitting the second na. The Hebrew places na both before and after the imperative refa: El na refa na lah ("God, please, heal, please, her"). LOGOS preserves both occurrences: "please heal her, please." The consensus follows LOGOS. (d) PNEUMA omits the vocative particle -- LOGOS has "O God" (a conventional English vocative rendering of El); EMET has simply "God." The consensus includes "O" as a vocative marker. (e) EMET has "unto" for el; the consensus uses "to." (f) The consensus adds an exclamation mark to convey the urgency of va-yits'aq ("cried out").

---

### 12:14 -- "If her father had but spit in her face" vs. "If her own father had spit in her face" / "would she not be put to shame" vs. "would she not bear her shame" (ve-aviha yaroq yaraq be-faneiha ha-lo tikkalam shiv'at yamim tissager shiv'at yamim mi-khuts la-makhaneh ve-akhar te'asef)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-said YHWH unto Moses: 'And-if her-father had-surely-spit in-her-face, would-she-not be-shamed seven days? Let-her-be-shut-up seven days outside the-camp, and-after she-shall-be-gathered-in.'" |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH said to Moses, 'If her father had but spit in her face, would she not be put to shame seven days? Let her be shut up seven days outside the camp, and after that she shall be gathered in.'" |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH said to Moses, 'If her own father had spit in her face, would she not bear her shame for seven days? Let her be shut outside the camp for seven days, and after that she may be brought back in.'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And YHWH said to Moses, 'If her father had but spit in her face, would she not be put to shame seven days? Let her be shut up seven days outside the camp, and after that she shall be gathered in.'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) For yaroq yaraq (infinitive absolute + finite verb): EMET has "had-surely-spit," LOGOS has "had but spit," PNEUMA has "had spit" with "own" added to "father" instead. PNEUMA's strategy of intensifying "father" rather than the verb loses the Hebrew's infinitive absolute construction; LOGOS's "had but spit" captures the emphasis on the verb. (c) PNEUMA renders tikkalam as "bear her shame" rather than "be put to shame" (LOGOS) or "be shamed" (EMET). The Hebrew niphal of kalam means "to be humiliated/put to shame"; "bear her shame" shifts toward endurance rather than the experience of being shamed. (d) PNEUMA adds "for" before "seven days" -- not in the Hebrew (which has the accusative of duration without a preposition). (e) PNEUMA renders tissager as "be shut" rather than "be shut up" (EMET/LOGOS); the additional "up" is conventional in English for the Hebrew niphal of sagar ("to be confined/shut in"). (f) PNEUMA renders te'asef as "be brought back in" rather than "be gathered in" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew niphal of asaf means "to be gathered in," the standard term for readmission; "brought back in" is a paraphrase. (g) PNEUMA uses "may" (permissive) where the Hebrew has the imperfect te'asef indicating simple future ("shall be gathered in"); "shall" (LOGOS) is more appropriate. (h) EMET has "And-if" for ve-im; LOGOS/PNEUMA omit the "and." The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 12:15 -- "the people did not journey until Miriam was gathered in" vs. "the people did not set out on the march until Miriam was brought back in" (ve-ha-am lo nasa ad he'asef Miryam)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-was-shut-up Miriam outside the-camp seven days; and-the-people did-not journey until the-gathering-in of Miriam." |
| LOGOS | "And Miriam was shut up outside the camp seven days, and the people did not journey until Miriam was gathered in." |
| PNEUMA | "So Miriam was shut outside the camp for seven days, and the people did not set out on the march until Miriam was brought back in." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Miriam was shut up outside the camp seven days, and the people did not journey until Miriam was gathered in." -- (a) PNEUMA opens with "So" instead of "And" (binding convention for va-tissager). (b) PNEUMA omits "up" from "shut up" (see v. 14 note). (c) PNEUMA adds "for" before "seven days" (not in the Hebrew accusative of duration). (d) PNEUMA renders lo nasa as "did not set out on the march" rather than "did not journey" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew nasa means "to pull up [stakes], to journey, to set out"; "journey" (EMET/LOGOS) is adequate; "set out on the march" is an expansion. (e) PNEUMA renders he'asef as "brought back in" rather than "gathered in" (see v. 14 note). (f) EMET renders the verse with a slightly different syntax: "the-gathering-in of Miriam" (using the infinitive construct); LOGOS renders "until Miriam was gathered in" (using a passive clause). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 12:16 -- "And afterward the people journeyed from Hazeroth" vs. "After that, the people set out from Hazeroth" (ve-akhar nas'u ha-am me-Khatserot va-yakhanu be-midbar Pa'ran)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-after the-people journeyed from-Khatserot; and-they-camped in-the-wilderness of Paran." |
| LOGOS | "And afterward the people journeyed from Hazeroth and camped in the wilderness of Paran." |
| PNEUMA | "After that, the people set out from Hazeroth and camped in the wilderness of Paran." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And afterward the people journeyed from Hazeroth and camped in the wilderness of Paran." -- (a) PNEUMA opens with "After that" rather than "And afterward" (the Hebrew ve-akhar begins with ve-, "and"; binding convention). (b) PNEUMA renders nas'u as "set out" rather than "journeyed" (EMET/LOGOS); both are valid for nasa, but "journeyed" is the standard rendering used throughout the Numbers itinerary notices and maintains consistency with v. 15. (c) EMET preserves the Hebrew place name "Khatserot"; LOGOS/PNEUMA use the anglicized "Hazeroth." The consensus follows the anglicized form for readability. (d) EMET uses a semicolon to separate the two clauses; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "and." The Hebrew has a waw-consecutive (va-yakhanu), so "and" is appropriate.
